From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

United States historic place

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Woonsocket City Hall | |
| [U.S. National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") | |

|  | |
| [Woonsocket City Hall is located in Rhode Island](/wiki/File:USA_Rhode_Island_location_map.svg "Woonsocket City Hall is located in Rhode Island") Woonsocket City Hall   Show map of Rhode Island  [Woonsocket City Hall is located in the United States](/wiki/File:Usa_edcp_location_map.svg "Woonsocket City Hall is located in the United States") Woonsocket City Hall   Show map of the United States | |
| Location | 169 Main Street, [Woonsocket, Rhode Island](/wiki/Woonsocket,_Rhode_Island "Woonsocket, Rhode Island") |
| Coordinates | [42°0′8″N 71°30′53″W﻿ / ﻿42.00222°N 71.51472°W﻿ / 42.00222; -71.51472](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Woonsocket_City_Hall&params=42_0_8_N_71_30_53_W_type:landmark_region:US-RI) |
| Built | 1856 |
| Built by | Albert B. Cole and Obadiah Slade (1856); Cutting & Bishop (1891) |
| Architectural style | Italianate |
| NRHP reference No. | [74000007](https://npgallery.nps.gov/AssetDetail/NRIS/74000007) [[1]](#cite_note-nris-1) |
| Added to NRHP | May 1, 1974 |

The **Woonsocket City Hall,** (also known as the **Harris Institute**) is located in [Woonsocket, Rhode Island](/wiki/Woonsocket,_Rhode_Island "Woonsocket, Rhode Island").

[Edward Harris](/wiki/Edward_Harris_(Rhode_Island_politician) "Edward Harris (Rhode Island politician)"), a leading Woonsocket industrialist, constructed the earliest part of the building in 1856, and it was known as the Harris Block, with stores on the first floor, and an auditorium hall seating 1,100 on the third floor. This brick structure has elements of Italianate styling, including round-arch windows and a heavily dentilled cornice. A major Richardsonian Romanesque addition, called the Granite Block, was made to the north of this structure in 1891. In 1902 the city purchased the building for use as city hall.[[2]](#cite_note-NRHP-2)

The builders of the original building were Albert B. Cole of Woonsocket and a Mr. Slade of Providence,[[2]](#cite_note-NRHP-2) probably Obadiah Slade, carpenter.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The 1891 addition was built by Cutting & Bishop of Worcester.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The architect of neither section is known.[[2]](#cite_note-NRHP-2)

The building served as the first public library in Rhode Island, housed on the second floor, which is now the [Woonsocket Harris Public Library](/wiki/Woonsocket_Harris_Public_Library "Woonsocket Harris Public Library").

In March 1860 [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln") spoke to a packed crowd in Harris Hall, which at the time contained one of the largest assembly rooms in the state.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

* [![Edward Harris, founder of the Harris Institute, the first public library in Rhode Island](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Edward_Harris_Woonsocket_businessman.jpg/120px-Edward_Harris_Woonsocket_businessman.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edward_Harris_Woonsocket_businessman.jpg "Edward Harris, founder of the Harris Institute, the first public library in Rhode Island")

  [Edward Harris](/wiki/Edward_Harris_(Rhode_Island_politician) "Edward Harris (Rhode Island politician)"), founder of the Harris Institute, the first public library in Rhode Island
